column2: Density Functional Theory, or DFT, predicts how atoms interact by estimating their electronic structure from quantum-mechanical principles.
DFT underpins much of computational chemistry and materials science, helping with areas such as drug discovery, energy storage, and semiconductor research [68,75,28,70,71,61,74].
Even though DFT is widely used, it is expensive enough computationally that it cannot be used as often or at as large a scale as researchers would like.
Machine-learning models may approximate DFT accurately while running much faster, with roughly linear scaling instead of DFT's much steeper scaling.
Here n is the number of atoms, so replacing O(n^3) DFT with a faster model can shrink a calculation from hours to under a second.
The ideal machine-learning interatomic potential, or MLIP, would work across the many different scientific tasks that normally rely on DFT.
In this paper, a task means both a chemical domain, such as materials, catalysts, or molecules, and the DFT settings specialized for that domain.
Within each task, DFT can be used for applications such as molecular dynamics, structure relaxation, vibrational analysis, and mechanical-response calculations.
Building MLIPs that work well across all those tasks is still unsolved.
In language and vision AI, scaling data and model size produced major improvements and broader generalization [2,25].
Scaling atomistic datasets is harder because DFT data are expensive to generate, so most atomistic models have been trained on smaller task-specific datasets [17,18,56].
One possible answer is to combine data from many tasks, creating very large datasets for multi-task training.
Recent public datasets for catalysts, materials, and molecules now add up to nearly half a billion atomic systems when combined [11,70,5,66,44].
That combined dataset covers a much wider range of interactions and chemical environments, as shown in Figure 1, making general-purpose atomistic models more plausible.
The paper introduces Universal Models for Atoms, or UMA, to test how accurate, fast, and general a single model can be across chemistry and materials science.
Training on about 500 million atomic systems creates new tradeoffs between accuracy, efficiency, and multi-task learning.
The authors study empirical scaling laws connecting compute, data, and model size so they can choose model sizes and training strategies that are optimal for training cost or inference cost.
To add model capacity without slowing practical simulations such as molecular dynamics, they introduce Mixture of Linear Experts, or MoLE.
They also use a two-stage training schedule: first pre-train efficiently, then refine the model so it conserves energy more accurately.

UMA is tested without task-specific fine-tuning and performs about as well as, or better than, specialized models in accuracy, speed, and memory use across materials, molecule, and catalysis benchmarks.

Reported highlights include state-of-the-art Matbench Discovery results [58], a 25% improvement in successful catalysis adsorption-energy calculations [43], and accuracy useful for drug-design ligand-strain energies [44].
The authors say they will publicly release the code and training data.
They also say UMA model weights will be released under a commercially permissive license, with geographic and acceptable-use restrictions.
An MLIP stands in for DFT, so it takes the same basic inputs: atom positions, atomic numbers, and sometimes spin and charge.
It predicts an atomic structure's energy, and other properties such as forces and stress can be computed from derivatives of that energy.
For molecular dynamics or structure relaxation, the MLIP repeatedly predicts forces, sometimes thousands or millions of times.
That is why an MLIP must be both accurate and fast.
UMA is a family with three sizes: small, medium, and large.
Table 1 summarizes those models.
The three sizes trade off accuracy and speed differently, so they target different use cases.
UMA-S balances accuracy and efficiency and is meant for costly workloads such as long molecular-dynamics simulations.
The source sentence is partially mixed with a figure caption; its main point is that UMA-M is the more general-purpose model and that MoLE replaces certain operations in the architecture.
This is a Figure 2 caption fragment showing the MoE and MoLE concepts.
The routing embedding uses only global information, so expert weights can be merged before the forward pass, which helps long rollouts such as molecular dynamics.
This is another Figure 2 caption fragment comparing UMA-S with MoLE against dense single-task and multi-task variants.
The caption notes that the MoLE model outperforms the non-MoLE versions.
The caption also refers to loss plots where the number of experts is varied from 1 to 128 for UMA-S.
This is the Table 1 heading summarizing UMA model variants.
This table/caption fragment reports inference-speed and maximum-atom measurements on an Nvidia H100 for periodic systems with about 50 neighbors per atom within 6 angstroms.
UMA-L is the most accurate DFT surrogate and is used mainly to probe scaling behavior and the current limits of feasibility.
The paper next introduces the UMA architecture.
It then explains MoLE, which increases model capacity and flexibility for training across multiple DFT datasets and tasks.
Finally, it outlines the training procedure for the UMA models.
UMA builds on eSEN, an equivariant graph neural network, and modifies it so the model can scale efficiently and accept charge, spin, and DFT-task information.
Standard eSEN takes atomic positions and atomic numbers and predicts total energy, per-atom forces, and optionally stress.
It updates an atom-level spherical-harmonic embedding through message-passing layers.
Those embeddings start from the atom's atomic number.
Messages pass between neighboring atoms within a fixed cutoff distance of 6 angstroms.
Each message-passing layer has an edge-wise block, then a node-wise feed-forward block, with residual connections.
The model normalizes activations after each layer.
The main edge-wise operation is the eSCN convolution [53].
After message passing, UMA expands eSEN by adding information about total charge, total spin multiplicity, and which DFT task should be emulated.
Charge and spin are integer inputs that let the MLIP handle charged structures and systems with unpaired electrons.
The DFT-task input tells the model which of the five training-dataset DFT settings it should imitate.
A single dataset is selected, and a corresponding random vector is supplied to the network.
This matters because the datasets use different DFT programs and levels of theory, such as VASP and ORCA.
The authors add a new embedding so UMA can include charge, spin, and DFT-task information.
Each of those inputs creates an embedding with the same size as the spherical channels; the embeddings are concatenated and passed through a one-layer feed-forward network.
The output is added to the L = 0 spherical-harmonic coefficients at every layer, meaning it modifies the scalar part of each atom representation.
The same embedding also helps compute the global embedding used for MoLE routing.
A common way to build general-purpose models is to train on more diverse data.
Scaling laws suggest that when datasets grow, model size should also grow to reduce loss efficiently [2,25].
The problem is that bigger models can become too slow or expensive for real simulations.
Mixture of Experts models try to improve that tradeoff by adding parameters without adding the same amount of computation [32,33,48].
In an MoE block, several expert modules each have their own weights and compute possible outputs.
Usually, the model sends forward a sparse weighted combination of those expert outputs.
MoE has worked well in large language models by improving efficiency and generalization [15,21,34,59].
Using MoE ideas for interatomic potentials requires extra care.
Unlike language modeling, potential-energy prediction is a smooth regression problem, and smoothness is needed for energy conservation.
The predicted forces also need to rotate equivariantly, meaning they rotate correctly when the molecule is rotated.
Therefore, the expert mechanism must not create jumps in the energy surface, and it must preserve rotational equivariance.
For MLIPs, a practical advantage is that the task and atom set often stay fixed during a long simulation.
A good MoE-style MLIP should exploit that fixed information to make repeated inference faster.
The authors propose Mixture of Linear Experts, or MoLE, for MLIPs.
In MoLE, the output is a weighted sum of linear expert outputs: each expert has weights W_k and contributes with weight alpha_k between 0 and 1.
This linear-expert idea appeared in early MoE work, but modern networks more often use sparse nonlinear experts that compete for routing.
For MLIPs, linear experts have special advantages.
First, using all experts densely lets the model share information and change smoothly between tasks rather than making hard expert switches.
Second, because the experts are linear, MoLE preserves rotational equivariance when inserted into the eSCN convolution.
Third, if expert weights depend only on fixed global information such as element composition, the merged weights can be computed once before a simulation begins.
By moving the summation in Equation 1, the model can precompute combined weights W*, making MoLE inference nearly as fast as a non-MoE model.
Equation 2 says the output can be computed as W* times x, where W* is the weighted sum of expert weights and the weights depend on system-level features such as composition, charge, spin, and task.
The model embeds those properties, concatenates the embeddings, sends them through a three-layer MLP, and uses softmax so the expert weights sum to 1.
The routing deliberately excludes information that changes during relaxation or molecular dynamics, such as atom positions or neighborhood geometry.
Training one model on many large task datasets is computationally demanding.
It is even harder for conservative models because forces or stress require an extra backward pass.
To train efficiently, the authors use a two-stage approach described in Appendix A.
In stage one, following earlier work, they train a model that predicts forces directly.
In stage two, they remove the direct force head and fine-tune the model so forces and stresses come from automatic differentiation and conserve energy.
This combines fast direct-force training with the smooth energy-conserving behavior needed for many simulations.
The authors also add several training-efficiency improvements in both stages.
Half-precision arithmetic is important for efficient modern GPU training.
As in LLM training, BF16 is much more stable than FP16 under automatic mixed precision.
Unlike LLMs, MLIPs are very sensitive to numerical precision, and BF16 alone can reduce accuracy by 20-50% depending on task and property.
The authors recover that accuracy by pre-training in BF16 and switching to FP32 for fine-tuning.
Training with many MoLE experts is also memory-intensive.
They add optimizations to bound and spread out memory use, described in Appendix A.
They also combine graph parallelism, fully sharded data parallelism for large MoLE layers, and activation checkpointing.
Together, those choices let them train models with up to about 10 billion total parameters.
Different datasets use different DFT settings, so their energy scales can differ substantially and complicate multi-task training.
The authors handle this with an energy-referencing scheme described in Appendix A.6.
That lets the model use one shared energy head across all tasks instead of separate task-specific heads.
A training set for a general DFT surrogate should include materials, molecules, and interactions between them.
OMol25 covers molecules, while OMat24 covers materials.
OC20 and ODAC25 are useful for molecule-material interactions, such as catalysis and adsorption.
OMC25 focuses on interactions between molecules in periodic molecular crystals.
Together, the datasets contain nearly 500 million training examples and more than 30 billion atoms.
Figure 1 shows that their combination covers almost all pairwise interactions between different elements.
The authors focus on large datasets because mixing DFT tasks is hard and much smaller datasets would probably have little effect on the learned weights.
All DFT calculations provide energies and forces by solving for ground-state electronic structure, but different chemical systems need different DFT settings.
For example, OMat24 uses the PBE functional with the VASP plane-wave code, while OMol25 uses omegaB97M-V with the ORCA localized-orbital code.
A single model that works well across such different settings is difficult to build.
Because the datasets differ in size and information content, the authors sample them at different ratios: 4 for OMat24 and OMol25, 1 for OC20 and ODAC25, and 2 for OMC25.
Appendix B describes the datasets and Appendix A describes the unified modeling strategy; the source then continues into a Figure 3 caption about dense and MoLE validation loss.
The Figure 3 experiments keep FLOPs fixed while varying model size and training data.
In that figure, diamonds mark the compute-optimal frontier.
Panel (c) plots training compute against parameter count.
The vertical green dotted line marks the estimated training budget of about 10^22 FLOPs, and horizontal dotted lines mark corresponding dense and MoLE model sizes.
Panel (d) compares compute with dataset size in atoms, and panel (e) overlays dense and MoLE compute-optimal frontiers.
The MoLE frontier is compared with a fitted power law relating validation loss to parameter count.
A compute-optimal MoLE model can match the dense model's loss with about 2.5 times fewer active parameters.
Appendix C gives the fitting details and parameters.
Table 2 reports mean-absolute-error results on held-out test splits for materials, catalysis, molecules, molecular crystals, and ODAC.
Energy errors are reported in meV, force errors in meV per angstrom, and stress errors in meV per cubic angstrom.
UMA is compared with available state-of-the-art literature results and strong baselines trained only on a single domain.
The table also gives approximate target accuracies for practical usefulness.

This continues the extracted benchmark table row for energy-related metrics.
This is another numeric table-extraction fragment from the results section.
This table fragment lists forces, energy-per-atom, and stress values; the heading was misread as a section title.
The source begins with table labels, then explains that power-law scaling relationships have helped predict optimal model and data sizes for fixed compute budgets in domains such as language.
For MLIPs, no previous model has been trained on a dataset as large as UMA's, and few exceed 100 million parameters, so it was unclear whether scaling laws would hold.
The authors find that UMA models show log-linear scaling over the FLOP range they tested, implying more capacity is needed to fit the UMA dataset.
They use those scaling relationships to choose model size and to show MoLE's advantage over a dense architecture.
Following scaling-law work, they study training compute C in FLOPs as a function of model size N and dataset size D.
They use total validation loss, computed like training loss, as the measured quantity.
They train a sweep of models with different parameter counts while keeping total training compute fixed.
Smaller models see more training data, while larger models see less, so the compute budget stays constant.
They do this for both dense models and 8-expert MoLE models, producing the iso-FLOPs curves in Figure 3(a,b).
The minimum of each iso-FLOPs curve is the compute-optimal model for that compute budget.
This fragment labels the optimal model-size discussion.
The authors fit the iso-FLOPs minima with power laws to estimate the best model and dataset sizes for a fixed compute budget.
Figure 3(c,d) shows that dense and MoLE models scale roughly log-linearly over the tested range of 10^18 to 10^20 FLOPs.

Given a 50-billion-atom epoch and an estimated pretraining budget around 10^22 FLOPs, the scaling analysis suggests that the largest compute-optimal dense model is about 700 million parameters, matching UMA-L.
In practice, the authors use the compute-optimal size as a starting point and train longer to lower loss while keeping parameter counts controlled.
They avoid too many epochs because overtraining can cause overfitting and break the clean log-linear scaling trend, so they stay within about two to three epochs.
This fragment starts the dense-versus-MoLE comparison.
By fitting the minima from Figure 3(a,b), the authors compare how large dense and MoLE models must be to reach the same loss.
An optimal MoLE model can match the loss of a dense model that is about Δ times larger; for UMA-M, Δ is about 2.5 +/- 0.2.
The advantage shrinks at very large sizes: a 700M-active-parameter MoLE model with 5.6B total parameters only slightly improves over the dense version.
Figure 3(e) shows this by overlaying iso-FLOPs curves.
As the parameter count grows, dense and MoLE models perform more similarly.
The authors suspect the dataset size, not the architecture, becomes the limiting factor.
This is the start of the multi-task versus single-task section.
To test MoLE for multi-task training, the authors compare UMA-S with MoLE against dense models trained either single-task or multi-task.
In the small-model regime, dense multi-task models always perform worse than single-task models.
With MoLE, UMA-S can reach performance comparable to small task-specialized models.
The authors also vary the number of experts in UMA-S.
Moving from one expert to eight gives a large loss improvement.
Moving to 32 experts helps less, and 128 experts adds almost nothing.
Based on those tests, UMA-S uses 32 experts.
For large models, multi-task training helps even without MoLE.
Figure 4 compares direct-force pretraining curves for UMA-L and same-size single-task models.
The metrics are normalized to single-task model results so multi-task performance is easier to compare.
Single-task models often overfit forces, while the multi-task UMA model does not.
UMA also reaches lower loss in most settings.
The exception is OMol forces, where both models are already very accurate, below 10 meV per angstrom.
Table 3 compares single-GPU simulation speeds for conservative UMA models against leading materials models such as eSEN, OrbV3, and MACE.
The speed benchmarks run on one Nvidia H100 80GB GPU using FP32 or TF32-high precision and torch compile when possible, excluding graph generation.
The test systems are periodic atomic systems with about 50 neighbors per atom at a 6 angstrom cutoff.
OOM means the model ran out of GPU memory.
Section D gives more benchmark details.
This is a Table 3 extraction fragment showing atom counts and steps-per-second speed numbers, including OOM entries for models that could not fit larger systems.
A core design goal is to support longer length scales, more atoms, and longer molecular-dynamics or relaxation simulations.
Because MoLE weights can be pre-merged, long simulations on one fixed system pay no extra inference-time or memory cost.
Despite having many parameters and strong accuracy, UMA models are competitive with specialized single-domain models in speed and maximum system size.
For example, UMA-S can simulate 1,000 atoms at 16 steps per second, about 1.4 nanoseconds per day, and can fit systems of 100,000 or more atoms on one 80GB GPU.
The models are also designed for multi-GPU graph-parallel inference, which could greatly speed simulations at the 100,000-plus atom scale.
The paper leaves multi-GPU inference for future work.
The evaluation has two parts: held-out test splits in Table 2 and practically important benchmarks in Table 4.
To cover materials, catalysis, molecules, molecular crystals, and metal-organic frameworks, the authors add new test sets and benchmarks alongside established ones.
The main text highlights selected results, with more detail in Appendix E.
This begins the materials-results subsection.
Table 2 reports performance on two out-of-distribution materials tests: WBM and high-entropy alloys.
The high-entropy alloy test set is introduced in this paper.
UMA-M performs about as well as state-of-the-art task-specialized models such as eSEN-30m-OMat.
UMA-L performs worse on high-entropy-alloy energies and forces, suggesting both overfitting and a difficult test set.
Table 4 reports materials benchmarks for thermodynamic stability, thermal conductivity, and vibrational properties.
For those public benchmarks, UMA models are fine-tuned on MPTrj and sAlex to match the benchmark DFT settings.
On Matbench Discovery, UMA-M achieves the highest F1 score reported so far.
All UMA sizes perform strongly there, suggesting that simply scaling model size further may not improve Matbench Discovery results.
The conservative UMA-S and UMA-M models also perform well on phonon-related properties, including metrics for thermal conductivity, free energy, and elastic constants.
This begins the catalysis-results subsection.
The catalysis evaluation uses OC20 S2EF and AdsorbML.
These benchmarks test adsorption-energy prediction, meaning the energy change when an adsorbate molecule contacts a surface.
Most previous OC20 models predict adsorption energy directly from the adsorbate's surface position.
UMA predicts total energy instead, once for the adsorbate-on-surface structure and once for the clean surface.
The adsorption energy is obtained by subtracting those values and the isolated adsorbate energy.
Table 2 shows UMA reducing OC20 S2EF adsorption-energy errors by about 80% relative to previous state-of-the-art models.
This may be because total-energy models better capture surface reconstruction than direct adsorption-energy models.
On AdsorbML, which asks for the global minimum adsorption energy, all UMA models beat the previous state of the art, EquiformerV2.
UMA-L improves the success rate by 25%.
That AdsorbML result suggests UMA could be practically useful for discovering new catalysts.
This begins the molecules-results subsection.
For models trained on OMol25, the authors used only the preview subset, about 70%, because the full dataset was still being generated when UMA training began.
Appendix E.1 reports UMA-S results trained on the full OMol25 dataset.
Table 2 reports performance on two difficult OMol25 splits: OOD-Comp and OOD PDB-TM.
UMA slightly outperforms eSEN-S-OMol, a strong baseline trained only on the preview OMol25 data.
Table 4 then examines two drug-discovery benchmarks: ligand strain energy and pocket-ligand interaction energy.
UMA-S beats the domain-specific eSEN-S-OMol on both drug-discovery benchmarks.
For strain energy, UMA reaches chemical accuracy, defined here as less than 2.45 meV per atom.
On distance-scaling tests, UMA does much better than the baseline for long-range cases even though it has the same receptive field.
The conservative UMA-S and UMA-M models also conserve energy well in NVE molecular-dynamics simulations using the protocol from [23].
This begins the molecular-crystals results subsection.
Universal MLIPs for molecular crystals have been less explored than those for materials, molecules, and catalysis.
The authors test UMA on OMC25 and on lattice-energy ranking and structure matching from the 7th Crystal Structure Prediction Blind Test.
UMA-S beats the task-specific eSEN-S-OMC baseline in every evaluation.
Its lattice-energy errors of 3 kJ/mol or less suggest UMA can often substitute for DFT in crystal-structure prediction.
This begins the metal-organic-framework results subsection.
Predicting CO2 adsorption energy in MOF sorbents matters for direct-air carbon capture and is an established benchmark.
As in OC20, UMA computes adsorption energy from total-energy predictions.
UMA matches previous state-of-the-art models on the in-distribution test set and performs best on the hardest ODAC out-of-distribution test, suggesting better generalization.
The related-work section begins by noting that MLIPs have improved rapidly, partly because larger datasets have become available.
In materials, Alexandria and OMat24 quickly improved results on the Matbench Discovery leaderboard.
Materials-trained MLIPs are sometimes called universal because they cover nearly all periodic-table elements, but they may not transfer well to molecules or surfaces.
One reason is that materials, molecules, and surfaces have different chemical, structural, and elemental distributions.
Another reason is that the appropriate DFT level differs across domains; the source sentence then merges into the Table 4 caption.
Table 4 also reports molecule and molecular-crystal benchmark results.
The NVE molecular-dynamics test checks whether the model conserves energy during simulation.
State-of-the-art literature results are included where available.
The caption notes that eSEN-S-OMol and eSEN-S-OMC were trained only on preview versions of their datasets.
For materials benchmarks, UMA models were fine-tuned on MPTrj and sAlex so their DFT settings matched the benchmarks.

This is a large extracted Table 4 row containing many benchmark values for Matbench Discovery, phonons, elasticity, AdsorbML, molecular crystals, molecules, catalysis, UMA variants, and literature baselines.
The preceding related-work point is that DFT choices differ across domains, for example PBE in materials and omegaB97M-V in molecules.
The authors argue that cross-domain training can learn unified representations and improve generalization.
Several studies have tried multi-domain MLIP training, but high-accuracy zero-shot performance remains difficult to prove.
One strategy is to pre-train on more than 100 million examples and then fine-tune for particular tasks.
Those fine-tuned models perform much better than training from scratch.
However, eliminating task-specific specialization would make the models much more useful.
Recent work suggests zero-shot transfer across two DFT tasks may be possible.
Scaling-law work studies how compute, data, and model size relate to one another.
In LLMs, scaling laws encouraged training larger models on more tokens because gains became predictable.
Scaling laws also help decide how to allocate a fixed compute budget between dataset size and model size.
Similar analyses have been used in MLIPs to compare long-run scaling behavior across architectures and training styles.
For example, prior work showed that equivariant models scale differently from non-equivariant models.
The limitations section states that UMA is a major step forward but still has open problems.
One limitation is long-range interactions.
UMA-S and UMA-M use a 6 angstrom cutoff, and although message passing expands the effective receptive field, separated molecules beyond that distance can still be problematic.
For example, an adsorbate starting 7 angstroms from a catalyst surface may be treated as independent from the surface.
Charge and spin handling could also be improved.
Currently, each discrete charge or spin value has its own embedding, which makes generalization to unseen charges or spins harder.
The authors note possible misuse risks and say they mitigate them by training on datasets aimed at socially beneficial chemistry and materials applications.
The conclusion says UMA trains MLIPs across many DFT tasks using nearly 500 million examples from several datasets.
By using scaling relationships among dataset size, model capacity, and loss, the authors train models competitive with or better than task-specialized models.
Simply making the model larger would make it accurate but too slow.
MoLE addresses this by increasing capacity while keeping inference efficient.
Within the model family, UMA-S offers a strong speed-accuracy tradeoff and can simulate 1,000 atoms at 1.4 nanoseconds per day on one 80GB GPU.
The authors evaluate UMA across materials, molecules, catalysts, molecular crystals, and MOFs and find strong performance throughout.
On established benchmarks such as AdsorbML and Matbench Discovery, UMA sets new state-of-the-art results.
The authors argue that harder benchmarks will be important for future progress.
Despite limitations, the results suggest one model can be accurate enough for practical research across a broad range of chemistry and materials science.
That supports the path toward universal MLIPs and new kinds of atomistic simulation.
